aliases: Breakfast burger with sausage and egg

english: Breakfast burger with sausage and egg
tamil: சாசேஜ் மற்றும் முட்டை காலை உணவு பர்கர்

region_of_origin: United States / Western fast food; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a sausage-and-egg breakfast burger on an English muffin. FOODON_00002473 (sandwich food product) is used as the closest broader class. A more specific term for breakfast burger or egg-and-sausage muffin sandwich is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers meat-based burger composite dishes. No specific breakfast burger or sausage-egg-muffin code exists in FoodEx2; broader code used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to a sausage and egg breakfast burger. SNOMED CT food concepts at this level of specificity are not available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard sausage and egg breakfast burger weighing approximately 160 g, consisting of a grilled sausage patty, one fried egg, and a slice of processed cheese on an English muffin.

nutrient_source.title: Sausage and Egg Breakfast Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F08020044 is sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite reference (USDA SR Legacy item 21081, pork sausage patty, combined with egg and English muffin components) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a sausage-and-egg breakfast burger on an English muffin in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous composite: English muffin (GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle. The sausage patty, egg, and cheese contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI of the composite. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 37.84) / 100 ≈ 20.8 for a 160 g serving (37.84 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 542 mg/100 g with low potassium offset (139 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this item; sodium at 542 mg/100 g (approximately 867 mg per 160 g serving) approaches half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and the low potassium content (139 mg/100 g) provides insufficient offset — request no added condiments and choose a wholegrain muffin to marginally improve the fibre and potassium profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate base (English muffin, GI ~55) with low dietary fibre (1.65 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should consume this item in moderation; the refined English muffin base produces a low-to-medium glycaemic response (estimated GI 55, GL ~20.8 per serving) and the low fibre content (1.65 g/100 g) does not blunt postprandial glucose rise — substituting a wholegrain muffin and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can reduce the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.3 g/100 g) from processed sausage and cheese, combined with elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease, this item should be consumed sparingly; the processed sausage and cheese contribute approximately 4.3 g saturated fat per 100 g and sodium at 542 mg/100 g, both of which are associated with adverse lipid profiles and blood pressure elevation — choosing a lean protein alternative and a lower-sodium preparation would reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed sausage containing sodium nitrates, saturated fat, and likely arachidonic acid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this item; processed sausage is a pro-inflammatory food due to sodium nitrate content, saturated fat approaching 4.5 g/100 g, and arachidonic acid from processed pork — substituting with grilled chicken breast or an egg-only option substantially reduces the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The sausage and egg breakfast burger is a Western-style fast-food breakfast sandwich consisting of a grilled pork sausage patty, a fried egg, and a slice of processed cheese assembled between a toasted English muffin. It is widely available at fast-food outlets, school canteens, and café counters in Singapore and across the Western world. The texture combines the soft, slightly chewy English muffin with the firm, savoury sausage patty and the yielding fried egg, while the melted processed cheese adds a mild, creamy saltiness. Nutritionally, a 160 g serving provides approximately 408 kcal, 20.4 g protein, 19.5 g fat, and 37.8 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high at 542 mg/100 g (approximately 867 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The refined carbohydrate base from the English muffin produces a low-to-medium glycaemic response (estimated GI 55, GL ~20.8 per serving), which is relevant for type 2 diabetes management. Saturated fat from the processed sausage and cheese (~4.3 g/100 g) is a consideration for cardiovascular health. The item is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following a halal diet without specific halal-certified preparation. It contains gluten (English muffin), egg, milk (cheese), and potentially soy (sausage binder), making it unsuitable for individuals with these allergens. Common meal contexts include breakfast, working lunch, and school canteen snacking.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sausage and egg breakfast burger?
Is a sausage egg burger healthy?
Can diabetics eat a sausage egg breakfast burger?
Sodium content of sausage egg burger
Is sausage egg burger high in protein?
Sausage egg English muffin calories Singapore

confidence_reason: Core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F08020044, provenance canonical, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite components as HPB does not separately report this field for the composite; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence below high given the strong primary source for all other values.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.3 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite reference for pork sausage patty (USDA #21081), fried egg, processed cheese, and English muffin in approximate proportions consistent with the described recipe, as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this composite item. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database for F08020044, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central to provide a defensible direct substitution for all these fields simultaneously. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on English muffin GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct measurement for this composite exists. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low; gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' accordingly. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; pork sausage is the primary protein component, making this item non-halal in standard preparation. 5. Cholesterol value of 18.22 mg/100 g from HPB source appears low for a composite containing egg and sausage; this value is preserved as reported from the canonical HPB source without adjustment.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Added mandatory local-language names (Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to local_names and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 and Q1.
Iteration 1: Added local-language aliases to the flat aliases array to ensure consistency with aliases_by_language (Q8).
Iteration 2: Corrected foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' as per validator feedback (G5).

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55, which falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the standard GI classification scale. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this correction explicitly.
